frame_analyses.description: Setup view — Penrose drain centered on red foam suture block secured with Velcro. Drain slit visible running longitudinally. No instruments in field.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Setup appears correct. Drain positioned centrally on block.

frame_analyses.description: Both needle drivers have entered the field. Right instrument (silver/gray) grasps the needle with suture attached. Left instrument (dark/black) is positioned for assistance. Suture thread visible draped across the drain.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Needle grasped at approximately mid-shaft. Both instruments entered field promptly.

frame_analyses.description: Needle held by right instrument above the drain. Suture loop visible above left instrument. Trainee repositioning needle for optimal driving angle.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Extended time repositioning needle. Multiple regrasps apparent between frames 2-4 suggesting difficulty achieving optimal needle position.

frame_analyses.description: Needle clearly visible (curved) held by right instrument tip. Left instrument working near drain surface. Suture thread anchored at drain. Needle being driven toward first mark.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Needle angle appears slightly oblique rather than perpendicular to tissue surface. Some hesitation visible in instrument positioning.

frame_analyses.description: Needle positioned near drain with both instruments actively engaged. The needle appears to have been partially passed through one side of the drain.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Continued suture placement. Pace is moderate — 51 seconds elapsed and still in placement phase.

frame_analyses.description: Both instruments working near drain at 71 seconds. Suture thread visible connected to drain. Needle appears to be completing passage through the second mark on the drain.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Extended suture placement phase (~60+ seconds). Significant time spent on needle driving through both marks. Some tissue manipulation of the drain edges visible.

frame_analyses.description: At 91 seconds, suture loops visible above instruments. Both instruments engaged in wrapping motion — suture being looped around one instrument to create the first throw. Thread tension visible between instrument tips and drain.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Beginning the first throw (surgeon's knot). The wrapping motion appears deliberate but slow. Suture loop geometry suggests double wrap being attempted.

frame_analyses.description: Both instruments near drain level with suture thread connecting them. Left instrument appears to have grasped the free suture end through the loop. Pulling motion to tighten the first throw.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: First throw tightening in progress. Instruments working close to the drain surface which is appropriate for maintaining knot tension.

frame_analyses.description: At 112 seconds, instruments repositioned. Suture visible connecting the two instruments above the drain. The pattern suggests wrapping for the second throw has begun.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Transition to second throw. Difficult to confirm hand switch from this camera angle. The instrument positions suggest the leading hand may have changed.

frame_analyses.description: At 132 seconds, instruments pulling suture. Needle visible on right side of drain. Second throw tightening or transitioning to third throw.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: The throw phases are consuming significant time (~40+ seconds per throw including wrapping and tightening).

frame_analyses.description: At 152 seconds, instruments pulling suture taut horizontally across the drain. Clear tightening motion. Drain slit edges being approximated by the suture tension.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Active tightening of a throw. Good tension application visible with both instruments pulling in opposite directions. This appears to be the third throw based on timeline.

frame_analyses.description: At 162 seconds, primarily left instrument visible with suture. Working near the knot site on the drain. Right instrument may be withdrawn or repositioning.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Completing the third throw. Only one instrument primarily visible which may indicate the other is holding tension or has been temporarily withdrawn.

frame_analyses.description: At 193 seconds, both instruments visible working near the drain. A different instrument shape visible on the right suggests scissors may have been introduced for cutting the suture ends.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Possible suture cutting phase. The right instrument appears thinner/different from the needle drivers used earlier, consistent with endoscopic scissors.

frame_analyses.description: Final frame at 203 seconds. Instruments withdrawn. Completed knot visible on the Penrose drain. The drain slit shows a VISIBLE GAP — the edges are not fully approximated. Suture marks visible on drain surface. Drain remains attached to suture block (not avulsed).
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Final result shows incomplete slit closure. The gap suggests either insufficient knot tension or suture placement deviation that prevented full approximation. Knot appears present but bulky.

knot_assessments.notes: Frame 10-11 show double-loop wrapping pattern consistent with surgeon's knot. Right instrument appears to lead the wrap. Confidence moderate — camera angle makes loop count difficult to verify definitively.

Frame 12-14 show wrapping and tightening for second throw. Instrument positions appear to have changed relative to throw 1, suggesting hand switch occurred. Confidence low on hand determination due to camera angle.

Frame 16-17 show third throw tightening. Final result shows visible gap in drain slit, suggesting the third throw may not have been sufficiently tightened, or the overall knot construct is not fully secure. Confidence low.

technique_summary: This appears to be a pre-training baseline performance. The trainee completes all required steps — suture placement through both marks, three throws, and suture cutting — within the 600-second time limit at approximately 198 seconds. However, the overall pace is slow with extended time in needle loading (30s) and suture placement (50s), indicating developing hand-eye coordination and instrument handling. The knot tying phases each take approximately 30 seconds. The final result shows a visible gap in the drain slit, suggesting either insufficient knot tension or suboptimal suture placement that prevented full tissue approximation. The drain was not avulsed, indicating adequate tissue handling.
improvement_suggestions: Reduce needle loading time by practicing needle grasping at the optimal point (posterior 1/3 of needle) with fewer regrasps — target under 15 seconds
Improve suture placement speed by developing a consistent needle driving angle perpendicular to the tissue — current 50 seconds should be reducible to under 30 seconds with practice
Focus on knot tension during each throw — the visible gap in the final result suggests throws are not being tightened sufficiently before proceeding to the next throw
Practice hand exchange technique to become more fluid between throws — each throw phase of 30 seconds can be reduced to 15-20 seconds with smoother transitions
Work on overall economy of motion — reduce instrument movements outside the immediate working area to decrease total procedure time
strengths: Completed the full task within the time limit with all three throws
Drain was not avulsed, indicating appropriate tissue handling and force control
Systematic approach — proceeded through phases in correct order without skipping steps
First throw appears to be a proper surgeon's knot (double throw) as required
